children.title: Self-Coherent Multisymbol Detection of Optical Differential Phase-Shift Keying
children.content: Moshe Nazarathy, Senior Member, IEEE, Xiang Liu, Senior Member, IEEE, Louis Christen, Member IEEE, Yannick K. Lize, Student Member, IEEE, Student Member, OSA, and Alan E. Willner, Fellow, IEEE, Fellow, OSA<|txt_split|>Abstract—This paper treats the recently emerging self-coherent multivector decision-feedback-aided detection techniques for optoelectronic devices. The goal of this work is to investigate both an optical preprocessing approach and an electronic postprocessing approach. We present in detail (i) the generation of a synthetic low-phase-noise effective local oscillator out of the modulation of the electric field, and (ii) the application of the hybrid (i) and (ii) the role of polypeptide (interleaved) DPSK in the optical preprocessing approach in easing the decision feedback realization process. In particular, we show that the proposed method can be used for general \(M_{\mathrm{f}}\) and differential quadrature phase-shift keying in particular; iv) three alternative integrated-optoelectronic receiver implementations for the optical preprocessing approach detailing the different processing strategies, including digital and electronic analog and digital building blocks; and (v) the principle, and alternative implementations, of the digital postprocessing approach to self-coherent detection. We finally compare the capabilities of the self-coherent detection and digital coherent detection techniques.<|txt_split|>Index Terms-Coherent communication, decision feedback, multiplexing, multiplexing of optical and multivisional PSK, optical communication, phase modulation.

children.title: I. INTRODUCTION
children.content: PON considering the possible benefits of coherent optical technology, an uneasy tradeoff stands out between its sensitivity and selectivity benefit versus its complexity of realization. It would be advantageous to develop differential equations that are more accurate than those obtained from the use of coherent detection, yet with substantially lower complexity, marginally exceeding that of conventional differential phase-shift keying (DPSK) [1]. We have recently introduced several novel detection architectures, referred to here as self-coherent, approaching coherent performance [2]-[6] yet incurs implementation complexity between that of a full coherent and a single coherent detector. In addition, we have also addressed front-ends entailing delay interferometers (Ds) with multiple delays. Related schemes using conventional DIs together with<|txt_split|>Manuscript received February 23, 2007; revised July 20, 2007. Published August 29, 2008 (projected).<|txt_split|>M. Nazarathy is with the Electrical Engineering Department, Technion-Israel Institute of Technology, Haifa 32000, Israel (e-mail: nazaray@ee.tech.net.ac.il).<|txt_split|>X. Liu is with Alcatel-Lucent, Bell Laboratories, Holmdel, NJ 07733 USA (e-mail: xliu20@alcatel-lucnet.com).<|txt_split|>L. Christen and A. Willner are with the Electrical Engineering Department, University of Southern California, Los Angeles, CA 90067 USA (e-mail: k.christen@ucla.edu)<|txt_split|>Y. K. Lize is with ITF Laboratories, Montreal, QC H4N 2G7, Canada (e-mail: yklyize@itflabs.com).<|txt_split|>Digital Object Identifier 10.1109/JLT.2007.912055<|txt_contd|>analog [7]-[9] or digital [9]-[11] electronic postprocessing, can also be characterized as self-coherent. Furthermore, the use of analog circuits in the proposed approach has shown the potential to compensate for transmitter, receiver, and channel impairments, thereby behaving in a similar manner to the conventional analog circuit.<|txt_split|>The self-coherent (SC) terminology indicates that an accurate phase reference is synthesized from the signal itself without additionally requiring a physical phase-locked optical local oscillator (LO) light source. Remarkably, the new designs approach the sensitivity advantage of coherent detection while simultaneously reducing the noise level. The high-quality synthetic LO is extracted from the received optical signal by averaging over prior symbols that are randomly modulated, yielding a beneficial phase-noise reduction effect through a linear combination of these previous symbols. The key for synthesizing an improved LO is to remove the data modulation on the past symbols through decision feedback (DF), e.g., by controlling the DIs in the optical preprocessing stage. In order to achieve this, we have developed a technique consisting of along with graphical and mathematical descriptions of the key process of aligning earlier complex symbols at discrete times \(k = 2, k = 3, k = 4,\ldots\) which participate in synthesizing an effective LO (improved phase reference) along with the previous symbol at time \(k = 1\). Data removal or phase realignment is performed by means of complex rotations using decision feedback, such that the rotated symbols become collinear with, and reinforce, the reference symbol at time \(k = 1\) as indicated. To achieve this, we have developed a technique consisting of applied phase reference with reduced phase noise, applied to the "self-coherent" detection of the current symbol at time \(k\).<|txt_split|>Notably, the SC principle pertains to differential phase shiftkeyed transmission over a window comprising multiple \((>2)\) symbol intervals, referred to as chips (a "chip" being the regular band interval over which the phase of the transmitted optical signal stays constant). Similar multisymbol DPSK (MS- DPSK) [12] decision-feedback-aided multisymbol phase estimation techniques [13]-[15] have been earlier considered in the wireless communication domain; however, their porting to a different channel is nontrivial, essentially due to the availability of phase information in the square-law detection process. Note that some special forms of MS-DPSK were realized without DF [16]-[18], albeit at the expense of implementation complexity.<|txt_split|>The DF SC MS-DSPKS alternative optical receiver structures are presented in Figure 1. The structure is shown in Fig. 2, and the differently abstracted wireless receiver structures [13]-[15].<|txt_split|>However, the processing required in the wake of square-law detection, the various optical and electronic realizations, and the interpretation of the improved reference phase estimation as self-coherent gain are novel contributions.<|txt_split|>Two main SC system architectures (in turn partitioned into subarchitectures) have emerged, respectively corresponding to preprocessing in the optical domain versus postprocessing in the electronic (digital) domain. The electronic postprocessing systems introduced in [7]–[11] under the name multisymbol phase control (MSPC) and the digital system introduced in [12]–[14] under the DfS) but require heavier electronic high-speed DF processing, either analog [7]–[9] or digital [11]. In contrast, the optical preprocessing architectures introduced in [2]–[4] under the name multichip/ DPSK (MC-DPSK) require more complex DI devices accomplishing most of the DF processing directly in the optical domain while reducing the complexity of the DfS in the electronic domain (which nevertheless is still somewhat more expensive than for conventional DPSK). A related recursive SC detection system was introduced in [19] based on a recirculating delay line.<|txt_split|>This paper is mainly devoted to nonrecursive optical preprocessing realizations of the self-coherent principle. Synthetic LO generation is interpreted in terms of estimation theory as a technique for estimating the phase shift keying (DIPPSK) and its contribution by an averaging effect over multiple symbols. The brief differential binary phase-shift keying (DIBPSK) treatments in [2]-[6] are extended to the more complex differential quadrature phase-shift keying (DQPSK) and, more generally, M-array phase-shift keying (M-MSK). In this work, we discuss processing SC architectures and their tradeoffs are compared. In Section II, we review a "polyphase" interleaved version of conventional DPSK. The SC principle is explained in Section III, rigorously modeling the attainable self-coherent gain, i.e., the reduction of phase noise power entailed in the process of synchrotronics. We also present some results on the performance of polyphase operation on esching the DF requirements and provides an overview of the alternative integrated-optoelectronic realizations, which are detailed and compared in Sections VI and VII. For completeness, the electronic postprocessing approach is briefly discussed in Section VIII. The final section concludes with Section IX. Finally, we conclude that the self-coherent versus the digital coherent detection techniques.

children.title: Fig. 1. Principle of operation of self-coherent detection: (a) DPSK (differential phase) detection, (b) Coherent detection, (c) Novel self-coherent detection.
II. POLYPHASE DPSK
A. DBPSK
children.content: The IFE of a conventional P-DBPSK contains a single \(\mathrm{D}[T,0]\) device generating \(\overline{\hat{q}}_k^{\mathrm{P}}[k] = \operatorname {Re}\{\hat{h}_k^{\mathrm{P}}\}_{k = 1}^{-1}\). The DBPSK rotation symbols are now real-valued, \(\hat{s}_k = \pm 1\) and the ML decoding law reduces to \(\hat{s}_k = \sqrt{\mathrm{sgn}\hat{q}_k^{\mathrm{P}}[k]}\).

children.title: B. DQPSK
C. \(M\)-ary DPSK, \(M > 2\)
III. SELF-COHERENT DETECTION PRINCIPLE
IV. SC DPSK RECEIVERS OVERVIEW
children.content: We now introduce integrated-optoelectronic realizations of MZD models. The MZD model is a supercell with multiple MZ DIs or more complex multiarm DIs, realizing<|txt_contd|>multiple delays spanning a longer observation window, and subsequent analog soft-processing of these outputs aided by delay.<|txt_split|>At the top level of description, all SC-DPSK receivers compose the following modules with appropriate interconnections: an interferometric front-end (IFE), a soft processor (SP), a slicer, and a decision feedback loop (DFL) (Fig. 2). In the case of DFL, the IFE is composed of a series of interferometric structures than in 2S-DFSK, and the IFE-SP cascade manipulates the received optical signal under the control of the decision feedback relayed from the slicer output via the DFL. The SP generates improved soft-decision sufficient statistics to be quantized by the slicer in order to generate the bit decisions. An advantage of all DF-sided DPSK systems is that the slicer is in fact identical to that of a 2S-DFSK system. This is because the IFE-SP cascade manipulates the transmission device(s) for DBPSK (DOPSK). This is in contrast with MS-DFSK systems without DF [16]-[18], which require considerably more complex slicers. The decision bit(s) provide the information output(s), namely, the estimates \( \hat{h}_{\mathrm{m - 4}}^{\mathrm{b}} \) and for DOPSK also \( \hat{h}_{\mathrm{m - 3}}^{\mathrm{b}} \) of the transmitted bits(s), representing the estimates of the transmitted rotation symbols \( \hat{s}_{\mathrm{m - 1}} \). The output bits are tapped to provide the auxiliary output(s) feeding the DFL. Notice the delay of the slicer decisions by one discrete unit due to the inherent one-clock settling period of the DFFs.<|txt_split|>For postprocessing SC-DPSK systems, the IFE coincides with that of 2S-DPSK. In preprocessing schemes, the IFE (Fig. 3) is more complex (but the electronics is simpler), comprising multiple relative delays \( T_{1} \), \( T_{2} \), \( T_{3} \), ..., \( (D - 1)T_{i} \), and \( D \) delay \( T_{i + 1} \). The IFE is a linear combination of a delay value (Sections V-VI) or alternatively realized as integrated multiarm DI devices (Section VII). For systems with<|txt_contd|>\(M > 2\) the DI devices in the IFE come in pairs providing "in-phase" and quadrature optical preprocessing. In the systems of Section IV, the DF is applied to the SP in analog form, which is then converted into a DC signal provided to the IFE, such that the DC degenerates to a simpler form.<|txt_split|>The improved SC DF-aided MS-DSPK receivers are compatible with conventional 25-DSPK transmitters. The only modification possibly required at the TX is the provision of polyphase operation, yielding a beneficial effect on easing the DP delay and reducing the latency. In order to achieve this, they become available one clock cycle later, i.e., \( T_{\mathrm{d}} \) seconds after the "current time" when the noisy decision statistics enter the DFFs in the slicer. Hence, when \( d = 1 \), i.e., without interleaving, the previous estimate of rotation symbol \( \tilde{\theta}_{\mathrm{m - 1}} \) requisite for the generation of the additive term \( \tilde{\theta}_{\mathrm{m - 1}} / \tilde{T}_{\mathrm{m - 2}} \) in \( \tilde{R}_{\mathrm{m - 1}}(16) \) must be hauled in zero time from the output of the slicer to the input of the DFF. This is achieved by using the addition \( \tilde{\theta}_{\mathrm{m - 1}} / \tilde{T}_{\mathrm{m - 2}} \) beats with \( \tilde{\tau}_{\mathrm{d}} \) at the current time \( k \) to yield the decision statistic entering the slicer. Fortunately, under polyphase operation, the decisions are still generated at the baud rate, yet the reference synthesis occurs at \( d \) times slower polyphase rate. The additive contribution \( \tilde{\theta}_{\mathrm{m - 1}} / \tilde{T}_{\mathrm{m - 2}} \) is only required after a delay \( T_{\mathrm{d}} \) relative to the current time, whereas the decision \( \tilde{\theta}_{\mathrm{m - 1}} \) has already become available \( T_{\mathrm{d}} \) seconds after the current time, provided that \( (d - 1)T_{\mathrm{d}} / T_{\mathrm{d}} \) seconds for the loop delay and the forward propagation to the DI output, enabling the transfer of \( \tilde{\theta}_{\mathrm{m - 1}} \) from the slicer output to the point where \( \tilde{\mathcal{P}}_{\mathrm{d}} \tilde{\mathcal{P}}_{\mathrm{m - 1}} \) enters the slicer input.

children.title: Fig.2.Top-level block diagram of self-coherent DF-aided MS-DPSK receiver.
Fig. 3. The IFE for the first class of self-coherent receiver architectures. (a) DQPSK \((\mathcal{M} = 4, \mathcal{D} = 4)\). (b) DBPSK \((\mathcal{M} = 2, \mathcal{D} = 4)\).
V. A FIRST CLASS OF REALIZATIONS
children.content: In this section, we detail the structure of the first realization class of SC preprocessing receivers. In this RX architecture, the IFE comprises \(2(D - 1)\) DIs for \(M > 2\) and \(D - 1\) DIs for \(M = 2\).

children.title: A. M-ary DPSK

Fig. 4. SP for DPQSK receiver using the first class of architectures (exemplified in Fig. 3) and the second class of architectures given by (29) (see Fig. 3). The DPL outputs are given by (36) (see Fig. 6).
B. DQPSK
children.content: We next specialize to the DQPSK format \((M = \mathbb{q})\), subsequently returning to DBPSK and to general \(M\)-ary DPSK.<|txt_split|>The SP (Fig. 4) now comprises a pair of analog adders fed by the two "main" DIs as well as by the outputs of mixed-signal circuits called complex-controlled inverters (CCIs). The CCI devices are each fed by auxiliary DI outputs and DF processed by the DFL. The array of CCI modules in the SP is intended to be used for active corrections to the two main DI outputs, which directly feed the two adders. The outputs of the two adders provide the SP outputs, fed into the two sign-decision (DFF) devices of the slicer.

children.title: Fig. 5. Mixed signal components of the SP. (a) Controlled inverter and (b) complex controlled inverter.
Fig. 6. DFL for DQPSK using the first class of co-sensor receiver architecture. The DFL is not directly labeled, rather the corresponding complex rotations are indicated.
C. CIs and CCIs
children.content:
A CI is a mixed-signal device essentially effecting multiplication by \(+/-1\) of its real-valued analog input, as actuated by a control bit input (Fig. 5) switching (maintaining) the polarity of its analog input when the control bit is 1 (0). A CCA is a linear combination of two inputs, and the outputs of CCI interconnected as in Fig. 5, implementing a complex multiplication of the pair of inputs \(\mathcal{I}(t) = \mathcal{I}^{n + 1}(t) + \mathcal{I}^{n + m}\) with<|txt_split|>\[
\sqrt {2} \beta = \pm 1 \pm j \in e ^ {j \pi / 4} \{1, \lambda - 1, - j \} \sqrt {2} \leftrightarrow \{0, 1 0, 1 1, 0 1, 1 1 \} \tag {30}
\]<|txt_split|>representing a \(45^{\circ}\) -tilted QPSK complex constellation symbol, mapped by the Gray-encoded control bit pair \(\{f^{R},f^{P}\}\)<|txt_split|>\[
\bar {y} (t) = y ^ {r v} (t) + j \bar {y} ^ {j m} (t)
\]<|txt_split|>\[
= \sqrt {2} \bar {s} ^ {\prime} \bar {x} (t) = (\pm 1 \pm j) \left(x ^ {r c} (t) + j x ^ {\tilde {m}} (t)\right). \tag {31}
\]<|txt_split|>As the CCI is a specialized complex multiplier with multiplicity \( m \) and a single non-zero coefficient, the CCI can be simplified than that of a general four-quadrant complex multiplier.<|txt_split|>To see how the SP and DFL operate, we specialize the results of this section to the case of a linear system with a linearized decision statistic (20) may be equivalently expressed as<|txt_split|>\[
w _ {k} ^ {(\alpha)} \equiv \operatorname {R e} \left\{\tilde {w} _ {k} w _ {k} ^ {(\alpha) *} \right\} = \operatorname {R e} \left\{\tilde {l} _ {k} ^ {\alpha} z _ {k} ^ {\alpha} \right\} \tag {32}
\]<|txt_split|>where we introduced a tilted version of (21)<|txt_split|>\[
\hat {q} _ {k} ^ {\prime} = e ^ {j \pi / 4} \hat {q} _ {k} = e ^ {j \pi / 4} \hat {r} _ {k} \hat {R} _ {k - 1} ^ {*} = q _ {k} ^ {r e} + j q _ {k} ^ {a n}
\]<|txt_split|>\[
\operatorname {a n a l} \tilde {S} _ {k} ^ {\prime} \equiv \tilde {S} _ {k} ^ {(0)} e ^ {i \pi / 4} \sqrt {\frac {(\pm 1 \pm j)}{2}} \tag {33}
\]<|txt_split|>with the transmission index \(\alpha\) discarded off \(g_{k}^{*}\)<|txt_split|>The decoding is still formulated as in 2S-DPSK as maximization over the \(45^{\circ}\) -tilled symbol \(\mathcal{E}_k^{\mathrm{max}}\) equivalent to making decisions on the signs of the variables, which are now the real part of the two parts \(u_{k}^{(0)}\), \(d_{k}^{(0)}\) of the tilted complex decision statistic (33).<|txt_split|>To see how the decision variables \( q_{i}^{\mathrm{rev}} \), \( q_{i}^{\mathrm{im}} \) are extracted by the SP, substitute the ref \( R_{k-1} \) (16) into (33)<|txt_split|>\[
\tilde {\eta} _ {k} ^ {r} = \tilde {r} _ {k} \left[ \tilde {P} _ {k - 1} + \hat {8} _ {k - 1} \tilde {r} _ {k - 2} \right.
\]<|txt_split|>\[
+ \hat {8} _ {k - 2} \hat {9} _ {k - 1} \hat {r} _ {k - 3} + \dots ] ^ {*} e ^ {j r / 4} =
\]<|txt_split|>\[
= e ^ {j \pi / 4} \tilde {r} _ {k} h _ {k - 1} ^ {*} + e ^ {j \pi / 4} \tilde {s} _ {k - 1} ^ {*} \tilde {r} _ {k} \tilde {r} _ {k - 2} ^ {*}
\]<|txt_split|>\[
+ e ^ {j \pi / 4} \hat {s} _ {k - 2} ^ {2} \hat {s} _ {k - 1} ^ {2} r _ {k} r _ {k - 3} ^ {*} + \dots
\]<|txt_split|>\[
= \theta_ {T} ^ {\prime} [ k ] + e ^ {i \pi / 4} \hat {g} _ {k - 1} ^ {*} \hat {q} 2 \pi [ k ]
\]<|txt_split|>\[
+ e ^ {j \pi / 4} s _ {k = 2} ^ {n} s _ {k = 1} ^ {n} j _ {3 T} [ k ] + \dots . \tag {34}
\]<|txt_split|>The first term \(\tilde{\gamma}_T^T [k] = \tilde{\gamma}_T^{(m)}[k] + \tilde{\gamma}_T^{(n)}[k]^T\) in the last line comprises the pair of outputs of the two main DEs, whereas the generic term in this summation (scaled by \(\sqrt{2}\) for convenience, is a constant). The other two equations) are amenable to generalization by a CCL Using (5), we have<|txt_split|>\[
\sqrt {2} e ^ {i \pi / 4} s _ {k = m + 1} ^ {*} \dots s _ {m - 2} ^ {*} s _ {k = 1} ^ {*} q _ {m T} [ k ]
\]<|txt_split|>\[
= \left\{e ^ {j \pi / 4} \hat {s} _ {[ k = m, k - 1 ]} ^ {h} \sqrt {2} \right\} \left(\hat {q} _ {l m T} ^ {v e} [ k ] + j \hat {q} _ {l m T} ^ {v i} [ k ]\right). \tag {35}
\]<|txt_split|>Indeed, the last expression is the product of two bracketed terms, with the right one comprising the outputs of a pair of DIs of delay \( mT_{\mathrm{d}}^{2} \) and bias phases \( 0^{\circ}, 90^{\circ} \) while the left one equals \( \pm 1 \pm j \). This leads to the structure of Fig. 4, with the SP containing \( D - 1 \) CCIs, each fed by an auxiliary DI quadrature pair, with the right NCI controlled by the term (5), in turn generated by the DFL by digitally processing the DFL from the input \( \hat{x}_{\mathrm{m}} = \left[ \begin{array}{cc} x_{\mathrm{m}} & -j \\ -j + 1 & 1 \end{array} \right] \), where \( x_{\mathrm{m}} = \left[ \begin{array}{cc} x_{\mathrm{m}} & -j \\ -j + 1 & 1 \end{array} \right] \), as shown in Fig. 6. The elementary combinatoric logic devices required for the generation of the rotation increments in the DFL are called QPSK constellation rotators (QCRs). The QCR building blocks realize complex multiplications of the form \( \hat{x}_{\mathrm{m}} \hat{n} = \hat{x}_{\mathrm{m},\mathrm{d}} \), where \( \hat{x}_{\mathrm{m}}, \hat{x}_{\mathrm{m},\mathrm{d}}, \hat{n} \in \{1, j, j - 1, j\} \) all belong to an unrotated QPSK constellation, essentially the four-element rotation group, with its symbols uniquely represented by the Gray codewords [000,11,11,10]. The QCR truth table is readily obtained and hence it is not reproduced here. In addition, the QCR matrix \( \mathbf{A} = \left[ \begin{array}{cc} 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 \end{array} \right] \) is also transformed into a matrix \( \mathbf{B} = \left[ \begin{array}{cc} 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 \end{array} \right] \) as sorbed in the CQR multiplier truth table (in actual realizations) and a pair of discrete-time delay lines also feature in the DFL.<|txt_split|>Lastly, we remark that the complex control inputs to the CCLs in the SP consist of \(45^{\circ}\) tilted rotation symbols \(e^{2\pi /3} \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} e_{\mathrm{max}} = -|V|\sqrt{2}\) as in (35), in contrast with the "straight" cumulative rotation symbols generated by the DFL (5). However no additional transformation is further necessary to map between the two types of symbols, since corresponding symbols (differing by \(45^{\circ}\)) in the two types of constellations are conveniently Gray-encoded by the same bit-pair.

children.title: D. DBPSK
Fig. 7. SP for DBPSK receiver using the first class of architectures (exemplified for \( D = 4 \)).
Fig. 8. DFL for DBPSK using the first class of self-coherent receiver architectures.
E. M-ary DPSK
VI. A SECOND CLASS OF REALIZATIONS
children.content:
In the next two architectures, the same principle of operation is at work: past reference chips are aligned by the feedback circuitry to add up collinearity, yielding a "cleaner" (higher SNR, lower PN) reference, prior to beating it with the current information. The output of the control circuitry is then used for the detection circuitry is simplified by eliminating the controlled inverters (CIs/CCLs), resulting in a less complex SP at the expense of more complex optical DI devices. All that is left in the SP module is a pair of analog adders (just one adder for DBPSK) used to apply the auxiliary DI outputs as additive corrections to the input signal. This allows the device to be able to reduce expense of resorting to more complex MZ DI devices, incorporating active electrodes. The DFL is now analog rather than digital, consisting of a driver applying band-rate electrical control signal to the active electrodes of the modified DiSs. As the CD/CC1 implementation may be increasingly difficult at higher and higher speeds \((40 / 160\mathrm{Gbps})\) while the electrooptic processing may be more efficient than the conventional DC/CC1, eliminating the CIs/CCLs, may turn out to be a worthwhile one.<|txt_split|>The SP complexity reduction is enabled by the insight that the phase rotations requisite for DF may be generated electrospun. The phase rotation is achieved by a symmetric decision feedback (IDF) feeding the past decisions directly into the DIs in the optical domain rather than into CCIs.<|txt_split|>Inspecting the balanced output of a \(\mathrm{D}[n]\Gamma_{\mathrm{c}}^{-1}\) device (9), and observing that the rotation increments are just phase factors, the idea is to put the phase-shift complex factor \(\mathcal{E}^{\mathrm{p}}\) to work in order to realize the mathematical operations of multiplication by \(n\) multiplications. In other words, occurring in the synthesis of the improved decision statistics (37) for \(M\)-ary DPSK with re-im parts expressible as<|txt_split|>\[
\delta_ {k} ^ {\mathrm {e v}} = \sum_ {m = 1} ^ {k - 1} \mathrm {R e} \left\{\delta_ {k} ^ {\mathrm {e v}} \right\} _ {(m - k, m - k + 1)} \mathrm {j} ^ {i \pi / \beta} F _ {k} (\delta_ {k} ^ {\mathrm {e v}}) _ {m - k}
\]<|txt_split|>\[
= \sum_ {m = 1} ^ {n} \mathrm {R a} \left\{\hat {\sigma} ^ {(f _ {m})} \hat {r} _ {m} ^ {*} - r _ {m} \right\} = \sum_ {m = 1} ^ {n} \frac {\hat {\sigma} ^ {(f _ {m})} \hat {r} _ {m} ^ {*}}{m !} [ 3 ] (3 9)
\]<|txt_split|>with \(\frac{\partial^n}{\partial m^m}\equiv \pi /M\wedge \delta_{[m - n - 1],m} = 2,3,\dots ,D - 1\) and similarly for \(\frac{\partial^n}{\partial q^m}\equiv\)<|txt_split|>\[
\hat {Q} _ {n} ^ {e m} = \sum_ {i = 1} ^ {2 n} \ln \left\{e ^ {(- i) _ {n} ^ {\prime}} \hat {R} _ {k} ^ {e m} \tau_ {n - m} \right\} = \sum_ {i = 1} ^ {2 n} \left\langle q _ {n, T} ^ {(e m)} \right\rangle_ {n} ^ {(m)} [ b ] \tag {40}
\]<|txt_split|>The last form of each variable in (39) and (40) features a summation of outputs of DIs with time-varying bias phases. This suggests an IFE structure containing auxiliary DIs with multiple delays \(T_{i} = T_{i}^{*}Z\), \(\dots, (D - 1)^{i}T_{i}\) wherein the phase biases are no longer constant (as in MZ DIs used in conventional DPSK). In the new structure, time-varying phase modulations<|txt_split|>\[
\gamma_ {k} ^ {(m)}, \gamma_ {k} ^ {(m)} - \pi / 2 \in \{\pi + 2 \pi / \beta \} _ {\beta = 0} ^ {M - 1} / M \tag {41}
\]<|txt_split|>are applied to high-speed electrodes incorporated in each of the auxiliary DIs. Assume a fixed phase bias component \(\pi /M\) is built into the DIs (an alternative would be to generate a DC component of the drive voltage to the modulating electrodes, onto which the DC current is generated). The DC component is superposed. The time-varying modulating drive voltage is then expressed in terms of the switching voltage \(V_{\mathrm{S}}\) as<|txt_split|>\[
V _ {d} [ k ] \equiv \left\{\frac {2 V _ {d} ^ {2} M}{M} \right\} _ {f = 0, n} ^ {M - 1}, \qquad (4 2)
\]

children.title: A. BDSPK

Fig. 9. Three-symbol DIPRK ( \(D = 3, \lambda = 2\) ) receiver using the second bit of the input signal in the \(10\mathrm{MHz}\) bandwidth. The output voltage \(V_{o}(0, V_{o})\) drives the voltage in the bit rates of the \(B_{\mathrm{th}}\) ,slicc decision bits at the siter slot. The voltage \(V_{o}(\mathbf{l})\) is applied to the electrode of the chip and the output voltage \(V_{o}(\mathbf{l})\) is applied to the output of which is summed up and added to the \(Z\) -delayed output of the main DC.
VII. A THIRD CLASS OF REALIZATIONS

FIG. 10. X-ray-boro-dip QDSF \((L^2 = 3, Z = 4)\) receiver using the second class of architectures. The DFL now consists of a driver generating the drive \(V_{\mathrm{D}}\) and a driver generating the drive \(V_{\mathrm{B}}\). The DFL is composed of two channels: the first channel is the voltage \(31 = 31 / V_{\mathrm{D}}\); the second channel is the voltage \(31 = 31 / V_{\mathrm{B}}\). The voltage \(25_{\mathrm{D}}(L^2 = 3, Z = 4)\) is simultaneously applied to both the in-phase and quadrature auxiliary DB of delay \(2.7\mathrm{s}\). The electrical balanced output voltage \(V_{\mathrm{D}}\) is applied to both the in-phase and quadrature auxiliary DBs at the same time.
VIII. THE ELECTRONIC POSTPROCESSING APPROACH
children.content: Herefore, we have thoroughly investigated optical preprocessing architectures. Alternative electronic postprocessing SC schemes were introduced in [11]. A simple yet universal digital implementation of the DF SC MS-DPSK receiver architecture was recently proposed for any multilevel DPSK formalism [27]. The optical front-end of the digital self-coherent receiver resembles a single-cell device with a single cell and is designed as required for conventional DQPSK demodulation. Derivations of the data-aided multisymbol phase estimation algorithms for multilevel DPSK and DPSK/amplitude shift keying were also provided for the digital implementation. When additionally including an intensity detection branch, this digital self-coherent receiver is also compatible with the recently introduced techniques reconstructing signal optical field [11], [27], [28], thereby allowing the detection of any multilevel phase and amplitude modulated signal [29] and digital compensation of transimpedance and harmonic dispersion [27], [28], nonlinear phase [30], and polarization-mode dispersion [30] in a similar way as practiced in the recently introduced digital coherent receivers [31]-[38].<|txt_split|>A comparison between the optical preprocessing and electronic postprocessing approaches is carried out in this section, with an emphasis on phase estimation, introducing a related new approach for the analysis of the signal. The main advantage of the iterative approach and providing a more streamlined derivation,

children.title: A. Optical Preprocessing Revisited
children.content:
We first briefly review this architecture, in the spirit of Section III, but in a notation more suitable for comparison with previous literature. The generic term in the summation (16) is denoted by \( \mathcal{A}_{\mathrm{max}} \), which is identified as an estimate of \( \hat{\lambda}_{\mathrm{max}} \) in terms of \( \beta_{\mathrm{max}} \), denoted<|txt_split|>\[
\hat {\lambda} _ {k - 1} ^ {(k - m)} \equiv \hat {s} _ {k - 1} \hat {s} _ {k - 2} \dots \hat {s} _ {k - m + 1} r _ {k - m}
\]<|txt_split|>with the subscript indicating the symbol targeted for estimation while the superscript indicates the symbol acted upon. In particular, we have \(\hat{A}_{\mathrm{t - 1}}^{\mathrm{c}} = \hat{r}_{\mathrm{t - 1}}\) . The buildup of the self-coherent noise is modeled by a single-order model such as multiple-time estimates for the noiseless reference \(\hat{r}_{\mathrm{t - 1}}\)<|txt_split|>\[
\tilde {H} _ {k - 1} = \tilde {\lambda} _ {k - 1} ^ {(b = 0)} + \tilde {\lambda} _ {k - 1} ^ {(b = z)} + \tilde {\lambda} _ {k - 1} ^ {(b = 0)} + \dots + \tilde {\lambda} _ {k - 1} ^ {(b = D + 1)} (5 0)
\]<|txt_split|>in terms of observations at times \(k - 1,k - 2,\dots ,k - D + 1\)<|txt_split|>Assuming the noiseless case—or, equivalently, taking an expectation—all estimate terms reduce to \(\left\langle \hat{A}_{k - 1}^{(n)}\right\rangle = \hat{A}_{k - 1}\) and the summation (50) of \(D - 1\) such equal terms yields a reinforced mean reference \(\langle \hat{R}_{k - 1}\rangle = \langle D - 1\rangle \hat{A}_{k - 1}\), whereas the noise in these terms builds up incoherently. Hence the difference in variance is reduced by a factor of \(D - 1\), as seen in Section III.

children.title: B. Electronic Postprocessing

Fig. 11. Five-symbol DBPSK \((D = 5, M = 2)\) receiver using the second class of architectures. The DFL now consists of a driver generating three drive voltages \(\{0, V_{\mathrm{R}}\}\) through the logic shown, consisting of XOR gates and delays.

Fig. 12. A five-symbol DQPSK receiver using the third class of SC preprocessing architectures, based on a novel DR device with interferometric feedback (Fig. 13). The receiver is split into \(n\) sub-channels, each with a lower arm of the interferometer by summing \(i_{k + 1}\) with phase-rotated versions of \(i_{k + 1} - i_{k - 1} - i_{k - 1}\), where the phase rotations applied by active phase modulation via electrodes led by three electrical transmission lines as shown. The LO signal is then converted to an output voltage in the middle of the transmission directional coupler, followed by balanced photodetection. The drive voltages generated by the DFL driver, corresponding to rotation increments requisite to generate the DC signal, are then used to convert the transmission lines feeding the phase-shifting electrodes in the two devices.

Fig. 13. Block diagram of the postprocessing algorithm (56). The indicated blocks are shown in red. The block diagram is a representation of the difficult component being the four-quadrant multiplier designated "mult" or "mult-". The output is the result of the multi-output operation of the two-dimensional valued connections, i.e., real-signalary pair. The system input is the pair of outputs of conventional IQD Dis converted in the digital image.
IX.DISCUSSION AND CONCLUSIONS
children.content: This paper was devoted to explaining the self-coherent detection principle, introducing and comparing several realizations based on the optical preprocessing and electronic postprocessing approaches.<|txt_split|>Below we compare the main attributes of the self-coherent detection versus those of the emerging digital coherent detection.

children.title: A. Self-Coherent Detection-Pros
children.content: - Eliminates the optical local oscillator and optical phaselocked loop required in coherent detection.<|txt_split|>- Can provide sensitivity approaching coherent detection.<|txt_split|>- Might not require polarization control or diversity (provided polarization independent DIs are used and polarization multiplexing is not used at the transmitter, i.e., a unique polarization state is being modulated).<|txt_split|>- Allows for the detection of multilevel DPSK signals [26], [29].<|txt_split|>- May allow for the digital compensation of chromatic dispersion in a single phase noise [30], and polarization-mode dispersion [30].

children.title: B. Self-Coherent Detection—Cons
children.content:
- May require higher analog-to-digital conversion resolution and more digital signal-processing power than a digital converter.<|txt_split|>- Requires optical filtering to reject noise and adjacent channel noise.  
- Requires the use of a high-quality (issue in most wide-length division multiplexing systems).

children.title: C. Digital Coherent Detection [31]-[38]Pros
children.content: - Allows the digital reconstruction of the signal field, permitting compensation of various transmission impairments such as chromatic and polarization-mode dispersion and nonlinear phase noise.<|txt_split|>- Provides wavelength selectivity through RF and digital filters rather than optical filters.<|txt_split|>- Provides the highest receiver sensitivity.<|txt_split|>- Applicable to arbitrary I&Q, not necessarily differential, modulated formats (PSK, QAM, etc.).<|txt_split|>Research challenges still outstanding are the evaluation of the quality of the research and the need to improve the work aiming at additional reduction of realization complexity.<|txt_split|>The precise noise analysis and error probability performance of the pre- and postprocessing self-coherent scheme is deferred to a future publication. The BER analysis will account for three key impairments affecting differential phase transmission over a fiber-optic link, namely, nonlinear SPM-induced phase noise, nonlinearity in the frequency domain, and noise in the spectrum. It will be seen that the two schemes (pre- versus postprocessing) have nearly equivalent performance, both approaching that of coherent detection. Hence, the selection of one scheme versus<|txt_contd|>the other ought to be driven by complexity-of-realization considerations, e.g., the complexity of integrated-optic front-end and analog electronics in the preprocessing case, versus the availability of high-speed analog-to-digital converters and the complexity of the digital signal processors in the postprocessing case.<|txt_split|>A related promising research direction concerns a class of SC realizations based on hard-detection [5], [39].

IEEE 2005, vol. 34, pp. 167-180.<|txt_split|>Mushe Nazarbay (SMN) received the B.Sc. degree in 2013 from the School of Electrical Engineering, Technion-Jerusalem Institute of Technology, Haifa, Israel<|txt_split|>He is a tenured associate professor with the Department of Electrical Engineering, University of Washington. He was a visiting associate professor with the same department. His research interests include information storage and data storage systems he held a post-doctoral position with the Information Systems Laboratory, Stanford University, Stanford, CA, USA. He also served as a Visiting Professor at the<|txt_contd|>Packard's Photonics and Instruments Laboratory, attaining the rank of Principal Researcher in the US, has been ranked as one of the leading research facilities for corporate CTO, and General Manager of the company's Israeli subsidiary. He is also a member of the Israel Technology Council, which he also serves as a Technology Venture Partner with Giza Ventures, which is and has acted as an advisor to the venture funds in Israel. He is also on the advisory board of five start-up companies.<|txt_split|>trial, under Prof. R. Tucker and T. Nielwalmas, teaching on PMSD emulation, at the University of Southern California, Los Angeles, USA. In 2014, he was a Visiting Professor at X. Liu on EPDSK optical packet encoding, and at the University of Southern California, Los Angeles, USA. In 2015, he was a Visiting Professor at X. Liu on IPSCS optical packet encoding correction. He is currently director of R&D for advanced modulation device at ITF Laboratories, Montreal, leading the development efforts in IPSCS and IPSCS-based communication systems.<|txt_split|>Dr. Lize is a recipient of the IEEE LEOS Graduate Student Fellowship in 2005 and 2006, the IEEE Merton Graduate Scholarship Award in 2004, 2005, and 2006, the SPII Scholarship in Optical Science and Engineering in 2005 and 2006, the OSA/Milton Chang student travel grant in 2007, and the Research Grant for Young Scientists from the Association of Physics, The New 2004 Focus Student travel grant, and the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 1137894) under the Postdoctoral Research Bursary in 2004. He has 15 peer-reviewed publications to his credit, as well as 35 international conference papers and five patents. He is a member of IEEE IEE's Board of Directors.<|txt_split|>Xiang Liu M-03-MU4 received the Ph.D. degree in applied physics from Cornell University, Baltimore, USA.<|txt_split|>Since joining Bell Labs, Holmfield, NJ, as a Member of Technical Staff in 2000, he has been involved in the development and implementation of communication technologies, including advanced modulation formats and detection schemes, fiber nonlinearity, and PMD mitigation. He has authored/co-authored over 100 research papers and book chapters and holds more than ten international patents.<|txt_split|>Louis Christen (M'07), photograph and biography not available at the time of publication.<|txt_split|>Yamick, K. (Liou 505) received the B.Sc. degree in 2001 from the University of Montreal, Montreal, QC, Canada, in 2001, the M.Sc. degree in 2004 from the University of Toronto, Toronto, ON, Canada, and the Ph.D. degree in 2007 from Ecole Polytechnique de Montreal, in collaboration with the University of Montreal, Montreal, ON, Canada.<|txt_split|>He has done internships at the Centre for Ultralight Materials, University of Sydney, Australia, and at the Sydney Institute of Technology, Australia, under Prof B. J. Egerton, Department of Electrical Engineering, Sydney, Australia.<|txt_split|>Centre for Ultra-Broadband Information Networks (CUBIN), Melbourne, Aus<|txt_split|>Alan E. Wilmer (S'87-M'88-M'91-F'04) received the B.S. degree in physics from Yeshiva University, New York, in 1982 and the Ph.D. degree in physics from Yeshiva University, Yeshiva, New York, in 1988.<|txt_split|>He was at AT&T Bell Laboratories and Bellcore. He is currently a Professor of electrical engineering, the School of Electrical Engineering (USCs), Los Angeles. He has 625 publications, including one book and 23 patents. His research interests include power electronic devices.<|txt_split|>Prof. Willin is a Fellow of the Optical Society of America (OSA) and was a member of the National Institute of Electrical Engineering, University of Washington, Seattle, USA, who has included the following: President of the IEEE Lasers and Electro-Electroics Society (LEOS), Editor-in-Chief of the IEEE-OSA JOSENAO of Lightwave Technology, The Journal of Electrical Engineering, and the Journal of Quantum Electronics, Cochair of the OSA Science and Engineering Council, The Royal Society of Electrical Engineers, Editor-in-Chief of the OSA, OSA Letters, Chair of the IEEE TAB Ethics and Conflict Resolution Committee, General Chair of the LEOS Board, and Member of the USC Board of Trustees Subcommittee on Student Affairs. He received the Ph.D. in Electrical Engineering from the University of Washington Fiber Communications. He has received the National Science Foundation (NSF) Presidential Faculty Fellow Award from the White House, the Packard Graduate Scholarship Award from the National Science Foundation, the Fulbright Foundation Senior Scholars Award, The LEOS Distinguished Professorship Award from the National Science Foundation, and the Teaching, Eddy Award from Pennwell for the Best Contributed Technical Article, and the Armstrong Foundation Memorial Prize for the highest ranked research student at the 2014 IEEE TAB Conference.
